Name: Blyth's kingfisher
Habitat: Evergreen forest streams, deep ravines in hilly regions
Best_seen_at: Namdapha National Park (Arunachal Pradesh), Eagle Nest Wildlife Sanctuary (Arunachal Pradesh)
How_to_identify: The largest in the Alcedo genus. Deep royal blue upperparts and rich rufous underparts. Features a heavy, all-black bill. Lacks the rufous ear-coverts of the Common Kingfisher; much larger and darker than the Blue-eared Kingfisher. Generally very shy and forest-dependent.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Royal blue upperparts, deep rufous underparts, black bill
Diet: Fish and aquatic insects; hunts by diving into fast-flowing forest streams

Name: Oriental dwarf kingfisher
Habitat: Densely shaded forest streams and small water bodies in lowland forests
Best_seen_at: Chiplun and Panvel (Maharashtra), Thattekad Bird Sanctuary (Kerala)
How_to_identify: A tiny, jewel-like kingfisher with a bright orange-red bill and legs. Upperparts are a mix of lilac-pink and dark blue-black; underparts are bright yellow. Only three toes on each foot. The 'black-backed' form is common in India, distinguished from the rufous-backed form by its dark mantle.
Diet: Insects (mantises, grasshoppers), small frogs, spiders, and occasionally fish

Name: Crested kingfisher
Habitat: Large mountain rivers and rocky streams in foothills
How_to_identify: Massive kingfisher with a prominent shaggy crest. Entirely black and white barred plumage. Lacks the blue/rufous tones of other species. Much larger than the Pied Kingfisher and lacks the clean white underparts, being heavily spotted/barred throughout.
Diet: Primarily fish; also takes crayfish and large aquatic larvae

Name: Pied kingfisher
Habitat: Lakes, rivers, estuaries, and irrigation canals
Best_seen_at: Keoladeo National Park (Rajasthan), Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary (Karnataka)
How_to_identify: Distinctive black and white plumage with a small crest. Known for its unique habit of hovering stationary over water before diving. Males have two black breast-bands; females have a single, broken band. Only large black-and-white kingfisher in the plains.
Diet: Mainly fish; also consumes crustaceans and large aquatic insects

Name: Blue-bearded bee-eater
Habitat: Dense broadleaved forests and forest clearings
Best_seen_at: Dandeli Wildlife Sanctuary (Karnataka), Thattekad Bird Sanctuary (Kerala)
How_to_identify: Large, predominantly green bee-eater with a square-ended tail (no streamers). Elongated light-blue feathers on the throat form a 'beard' often visible when calling. Much larger and less gregarious than Merops bee-eaters. Large, decurved bill.
Diet: Large bees, wasps, and hornets; often hunts from high forest canopies

Name: Asian green bee-eater
Habitat: Open country, scrub, farmland, and gardens
Best_seen_at: Widespread across India; common in Sultanpur (Haryana), Keoladeo (Rajasthan)
How_to_identify: Small, slender green bird with long central tail streamers. Features a black eye-stripe and a thin black 'gorget' on the throat. Indian subspecies has a rufous-tinged crown and a blue wash on the chin/throat. Frequently perches on wires and low bushes.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Bright emerald green, blue throat/chin, rufous crown
Diet: Bees, wasps, ants, and other flying insects caught in mid-air

Name: Blue-cheeked bee-eater
Habitat: Semi-deserts, saline pans, and open plains near water
Best_seen_at: Little Rann of Kutch (Gujarat), Kanyakumari saltpans (Tamil Nadu - breeding)
How_to_identify: Larger than the Asian Green Bee-eater. Predominantly green with distinctive blue 'cheeks' (supercilium and malar area). Yellow throat with a prominent chestnut-brown patch below it. Long tail streamers. Distinguished from Blue-tailed by the blue face and green (not blue) rump.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Green, blue cheeks, yellow and chestnut throat

Name: Blue-tailed bee-eater
Habitat: Near large water bodies, river valleys, and coastal wetlands
How_to_identify: Large green bee-eater with a conspicuous blue rump and tail. Throat is yellow merging into a broad chestnut patch. Tail has long central streamers. Similar to Blue-cheeked but has a greener face and a distinctly blue tail and rump.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Green, blue tail/rump, chestnut and yellow throat
Diet: Bees, dragonflies, and butterflies; frequently forages over water

Name: Blue-throated bee-eater
Habitat: Mangroves, forest edges, and coastal scrub
Best_seen_at: Campbell Bay (Great Nicobar Island); extremely rare vagrant to mainland India
How_to_identify: Striking combination of a deep chocolate-maroon crown and nape with a brilliant blue throat. Upperparts are green and the tail is blue with streamers. In India, primarily restricted to the Nicobar Islands. Maroon crown is diagnostic compared to other Indian bee-eaters.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Maroon crown/nape, blue throat, green wings

Name: Chestnut-headed bee-eater
Habitat: Open forests, forest edges, and wooded foothills
Best_seen_at: Thattekad Bird Sanctuary (Kerala), Anamalai Hills (Tamil Nadu), Jim Corbett (Uttarakhand)
How_to_identify: Green body with a rich chestnut crown, nape, and upper back. Throat is bright yellow with a thin black line below it. Unlike most other Merops, it lacks elongated central tail streamers. Rump is pale blue. Smaller than Blue-tailed.
Primary_colors_of_the_bird: Chestnut head, yellow throat, green body, blue rump
Diet: Bees and wasps; often forages in small parties from high perches
